Q1: I tried to build a consistent routine, honestly I did. I'd block out time in the evenings after my part-time job, usually around 4-5 hours on weekdays when I could manage it. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd often pace around my room while reviewing notes or work through practice problems by hand. The challenge was always having enough energy left after work and other responsibilities, but I kept pushing through because I knew I needed to put in the hours.
Q2: My pattern definitely shifted based on what was due. Early in the semester I tried to keep regular Tuesday and Thursday evening study blocks, plus weekends. But when assignments piled up or when I had extra shifts at work, everything got compressed toward deadlines. I'd end up doing marathon sessions right before things were due, which wasn't ideal but felt like the only option given my situation.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new content had been posted or if there were updates about assignments. I logged in pretty frequently - almost daily during heavy weeks - because I was anxious about missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos again because reading alone doesn't stick for me. The discussion boards also pulled me in since I found talking through concepts with classmates helped solidify things.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. It happened around week three when I realized how much I was relying on the video content to understand material. I'd wake up, check the LMS before my morning shift, then again during lunch break on my phone. The anxiety of possibly missing an announcement made it feel necessary rather than optional.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me - I watched over 200 minutes worth because I needed to see concepts demonstrated, not just read about them. The discussion forums were also valuable because explaining things to peers or reading their questions helped me process information differently. I also appreciated any interactive elements or practice problems because I learn by doing, not by passively reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skimmed the lengthy reading materials or PDF documents. With limited time and energy, I prioritized videos and hands-on activities over dense text. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant if I'd already watched the lecture covering the same content. I knew I should engage with everything, but I had to make choices about where to invest my limited study time.
Q7: An activity felt worthwhile when I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could practice something and feel myself getting better at it, that motivated me to continue. The discussion posts felt valuable because I was actively processing information, not just consuming it. Anything that let me move, write, or interact rather than just read felt like time well spent.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With work, limited resources, and the anxiety of just trying to pass, I focused almost entirely on required components. I'd tell myself I'd come back to optional materials if I had time, but that time rarely materialized. Required activities had clear stakes attached, which honestly helped me prioritize when everything felt overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study during my commute or during slow periods at work. I'd write out problems by hand in a notebook and work through them physically - that's how I actually learn. I also used free YouTube videos and online resources to supplement concepts I was struggling with since I couldn't afford textbooks or tutoring.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a video once, then rewatch it three or four times offline on my phone. I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook, physically writing and rewriting solutions until they made sense. I also studied with a couple classmates in person, talking through concepts over coffee. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Constantly. I'd access a lecture video or download slides once, then that material would live on my phone or laptop for weeks. I'd review those materials during bus rides, work breaks, or late at night when I didn't have reliable internet. The LMS would show one access, but I probably engaged with that content five or six times in different contexts.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My work schedule increased, I was exhausted, and the material was getting more complex. The anxiety would spike and sometimes I'd freeze up, spending time in the LMS but not really absorbing anything. There were nights I'd stare at the screen feeling scared about falling behind but too mentally drained to actually process the content.
Q13: My job was the biggest competing factor - I need it to pay for school and living expenses, but it ate into study time constantly. When I was exhausted after a long shift, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but end up just clicking through things without real focus. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections temporarily, which probably hurt me in the long run.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. There were sessions where I'd have the LMS open for an hour, watching videos or scrolling through materials, but my brain was just... elsewhere. The anxiety made it hard to concentrate sometimes. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't really learning anything meaningful in those moments.
Q15: The exam preparation materials scared me, so I kept putting them off. I knew they were important, but facing them meant confronting how much I didn't understand. I'd tell myself I'd get to them after finishing other tasks, but then I'd run out of time. It's frustrating because I knew better, but the fear of failure sometimes paralyzed me into avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my login frequency would spike. The days right before something was due, I'd be in the system constantly, trying to cram in everything I'd been meaning to do. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of deadlines was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and force action.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. My assignment scores were actually pretty decent, which kept me hopeful even when exam prep felt overwhelming. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and sometimes I'd avoid checking the LMS for a day or two after submitting something because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways - having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within. But the pacing sometimes felt relentless given my other commitments. I appreciated when content built logically on previous material, but when I fell behind on one section, it created a snowball effect that was hard to recover from.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand was crucial for someone with my schedule. Being able to pause, rewind, and rewatch made a huge difference. What made things harder was when important information was buried in long text documents - I'd miss things because I couldn't engage with that format effectively. More interactive, hands-on elements would have helped me tremendously.
Q20: That was literally me. I was highly active in the LMS - logging in frequently, watching tons of videos, participating in discussions - but my assignment completion was terrible and my exam score reflected that. I think it shows someone who's trying hard, engaging with content, but struggling to translate that engagement into finished work. Fear, time constraints, and difficulty with the exam format all played roles for me.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone learns efficiently offline or already has strong background knowledge, they might not need to spend as much time in the LMS. For me, I needed the LMS heavily, but I know classmates who'd briefly check materials then study from textbooks or their own notes. The LMS activity doesn't capture natural learning ability or efficient study strategies.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone's active, what they're clicking on, how long they spend on pages. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline learning. They can't see me pacing my room reciting concepts, working problems in my notebook, or having study discussions with friends. They show behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content - it's wrestling with difficult concepts, asking questions, trying to connect ideas. For me, engagement looks like watching a video multiple times until it clicks, discussing problems with classmates, and keeping at it even when I'm scared of failing.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting to succeed despite limited resources and high anxiety. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the emotional toll of balancing work and school, or the genuine effort behind every login. My final grade says 'fail,' but that doesn't reflect someone who gave up - it reflects someone who was climbing uphill the whole time and just couldn't quite reach the summit this semester.
